units.name: Neural layers, self-organization, and activation choices
units.description: Introduces assignment context, revisits feed-forward neural layers, and surveys why differentiable activations replaced threshold units.
units.summary: The instructor frames assignment 2 as a combined exercise in neural-network math, dependency parsing, and first exposure to PyTorch [ts=50s][ts=87s]. He then revisits multilayer neural networks as cascades of logistic-regression-like units whose hidden layers learn useful intermediate representations for downstream decisions rather than relying only on hand-designed features [ts=243s][ts=285s]. The layer computation is written in vectorized form as z = Wx + b followed by an elementwise activation function f(z) [ts=394s][ts=420s]. He motivates differentiable nonlinearities by contrasting them with threshold units, which have no usable slope for gradient-based learning [ts=515s][ts=550s], and then surveys common choices—sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, leaky ReLU, and newer ReLU-like alternatives—highlighting the trade-off between dead regions and easier gradient flow [ts=590s][ts=690s][ts=813s][ts=847s].

key_points.text: Assignment 2 is meant to build neural-network math intuition while also introducing dependency parsing and PyTorch.
Hidden layers let the network self-organize intermediate representations that are useful for later computations.
A layer is computed as z = Wx + b followed by an elementwise activation function h = f(z).
Threshold activations make learning hard because flat regions provide no gradient.
ReLU became popular because its positive-region slope is always 1, making backward gradient flow simple and effective despite dead negative regions.

units.name: Function approximation, gradients, Jacobians, and chain rule
units.description: Explains why nonlinearities are required and builds the multivariable-calculus toolkit needed for gradient-based learning.
units.summary: This segment first argues that neural networks are function approximators and that nonlinearities are essential because a stack of pure matrix multiplies collapses to a single linear transform, giving no extra representational power [ts=903s][ts=939s][ts=952s]. The lecture then introduces stochastic gradient descent as the core learning update and asks how to compute gradients for arbitrary composed functions [ts=1028s][ts=1046s]. To make that possible, multivariable calculus is reframed as single-variable calculus carried out with vectors and matrices [ts=1228s]. The instructor reviews scalar derivatives, then defines gradients as vectors of partial derivatives for scalar-output functions and Jacobians as matrices of partial derivatives for multi-output functions [ts=1388s][ts=1400s][ts=1463s]. The section ends by showing that composition is handled by the chain rule, with Jacobians multiplying just as ordinary derivatives do in the scalar case [ts=1536s][ts=1615s].

key_points.text: Without nonlinearities, composing multiple matrix multiplies is equivalent to a single linear transform, so depth adds no representational power.
Learning is presented through the stochastic gradient descent update using the gradient of the objective function.
For a scalar-output function with n inputs, the gradient is the vector of partial derivatives with respect to each input.
For a function with n inputs and m outputs, the Jacobian is the matrix of output-by-input partial derivatives.
For composed multivariable functions, the chain rule multiplies the relevant Jacobians.

units.name: Vectorized derivatives for a neural-network layer
units.description: Derives Jacobians and parameter gradients for activations, affine layers, and the score function, including delta reuse and shape conventions.
units.summary: The lecture now derives the gradients for specific neural-network building blocks. For an elementwise activation h = f(z), the Jacobian is diagonal because each output coordinate depends only on the matching input coordinate, so the diagonal entries are f'(z_i) and off-diagonal entries are zero [ts=1644s][ts=1715s]. For affine computation z = Wx + b, the derivative with respect to x is W and the derivative with respect to b is the identity matrix [ts=1763s][ts=1809s]. Using the small network example, the score derivative is expanded with the chain rule as ds/db = ds/dh · dh/dz · dz/db [ts=1997s], which simplifies using a Hadamard product for elementwise multiplication [ts=2081s]. The instructor then names the shared upstream gradient/error signal δ so it can be reused across parameter derivatives instead of recomputed [ts=2206s], derives the weight gradient as an outer-product style combination of upstream gradient and local input [ts=2417s], and warns that practical implementations follow a shape convention so gradient tensors match the shapes of the parameters they update [ts=2594s][ts=2620s].

key_points.text: The Jacobian of an elementwise activation is diagonal, with f'(z_i) on the diagonal and zeros elsewhere.
For z = Wx + b, the derivative with respect to x is W.
For z = Wx + b, the derivative with respect to b is the identity matrix.
The score derivative with respect to the bias is decomposed as ds/db = ds/dh times dh/dz times dz/db.
The Hadamard product denotes elementwise multiplication in neural-network gradient formulas.
The shared upstream gradient delta should be computed once and reused across ds/db and ds/dW calculations.
The weight gradient is formed from the upstream gradient and the local input in an outer-product pattern.
The shape convention stores gradients in the same shapes as their corresponding parameter tensors.

units.name: Backpropagation on computation graphs
units.description: Recasts neural networks as computation graphs and derives backward-pass rules from local gradients and branch structure.
units.summary: Backpropagation is distilled to two ideas: apply the chain rule and store intermediate results so repeated work is avoided [ts=2711s][ts=2733s]. The network is redrawn as a computation graph whose forward pass computes values node by node—for example x to Wx to z to h to s [ts=2776s][ts=2811s]. The backward pass starts from ds/ds = 1 and sends gradients backward by multiplying each node's upstream gradient by its local gradient [ts=2867s][ts=2947s]. The instructor then uses a toy graph f(x,y,z) = (x + y) * max(y,z) with x = 1, y = 2, z = 0 to show how forward values are computed [ts=3044s][ts=3083s], how local derivatives are assigned for plus, max, and multiply [ts=3125s][ts=3191s], and why branch outputs require summing incoming gradient contributions at shared variables [ts=3379s][ts=3412s].

key_points.text: Backpropagation is just the chain rule plus caching of intermediate results.
A neural network can be represented as a computation graph whose source nodes are inputs and whose interior nodes are operations.
The backward pass starts from ds/ds = 1 and computes downstream gradients as upstream gradient times local gradient.
For nodes with multiple inputs, a separate local gradient is taken with respect to each input.
In the toy graph example, the forward pass evaluates f(x,y,z) = (x + y) * max(y,z) to 6 when x=1, y=2, z=0.
At graph branches, gradient contributions from different paths are summed at the shared variable.

units.name: Efficient reverse-mode autodiff and gradient checking
units.description: Generalizes backpropagation to arbitrary DAGs, connects it to autodiff frameworks, and finishes with practical gradient checking.
units.summary: The final segment generalizes backpropagation from layered networks to any acyclic computation graph. The algorithm topologically sorts nodes for the forward pass and then visits them in reverse order for the backward pass, propagating gradients with upstream-times-local computations [ts=3616s][ts=3646s][ts=3693s]. A correct implementation should have backward complexity in the same Big-O class as the forward calculation, because extra cost usually means recomputation [ts=3736s]. The lecture then explains how modern frameworks manage the computation graph automatically but still expect humans to specify forward behavior and local derivatives for new operations or layers, typically caching forward inputs for use during backward [ts=3866s][ts=3968s][ts=4065s]. It closes with numeric gradient checking via the central-difference estimate (f(x+h) - f(x-h)) / (2h), recommending h around 10^-4 and rough agreement within about 10^-2 when validating a backward implementation [ts=4119s][ts=4175s][ts=4198s]. The wrap-up restates that backprop is efficient chain-rule application and motivates understanding it to debug later phenomena such as exploding and vanishing gradients [ts=4285s][ts=4368s].

key_points.text: Forward propagation on a general computation graph proceeds in topological order over a DAG.
Backward propagation visits nodes in reverse topological order and computes downstream gradients from upstream gradients and local gradients.
If implemented correctly, backward propagation should have the same Big-O complexity class as the forward computation.
Modern deep-learning frameworks manage the computation graph but rely on user-defined forward and local-gradient logic for custom operations.
A common implementation trick is to cache forward inputs so the backward method can use them when computing gradients.
Numeric gradient checking uses the central-difference estimate (f(x+h) - f(x-h)) / (2h), often with h around 10^-4.
Backpropagation is the chain rule applied efficiently.
